**Cape Verde**[[a]](#cite_note-9) or **Cabo Verde**,[[b]](#cite_note-10)[[c]](#cite_note-11) officially the **Republic of Cabo Verde**,[[d]](#cite_note-12) is an [island country](/wiki/Island_country "Island country") and [archipelagic state](/wiki/Archipelagic_state "Archipelagic state") in the central [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") off the coast of [West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa"). It consists of ten [volcanic islands](/wiki/Volcanic_island "Volcanic island") with a combined land area of about 4,033 square kilometres (1,557 sq mi).[[9]](#cite_note-NameNatGeo-13) These islands lie between 600 and 850 kilometres (370 and 530 miles) west of [Cap-Vert](/wiki/Cap-Vert "Cap-Vert"), the westernmost point of [continental Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), after which it is named.[[10]](#cite_note-14) Cape Verde forms part of the [Macaronesia](/wiki/Macaronesia "Macaronesia") ecoregion, along with the [Azores](/wiki/Azores "Azores"), the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands"), [Madeira](/wiki/Madeira "Madeira") and the [Savage Isles](/wiki/Savage_Isles "Savage Isles").

The archipelago was uninhabited until the 15th century, when [Portuguese explorers](/wiki/Portuguese_explorer "Portuguese explorer") settled the islands, establishing one of the first [European settlements](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery") in the [tropics](/wiki/Tropics "Tropics"). Its strategic position gave it a significant role in the [transatlantic slave trade](/wiki/Transatlantic_slave_trade "Transatlantic slave trade") during the 16th and 17th centuries; the islands saw rapid economic growth driven by the trade of manufactured goods, rum, and cloth for African slaves, ivory, and gold. By the mid 19th century, increased foreign competition, persistent drought, and the decline of the slave trade led to economic decline and emigration; Cape Verde gradually recovered as an important commercial centre and stopping point for major shipping routes.

Cape Verde became independent in 1975. Since the early 1990s, it has been a stable [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") and has remained one of the most developed and democratic countries in Africa. Lacking natural resources, its [developing economy](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country") is mostly service-oriented, with a growing focus on tourism and foreign investment. With a population of around 491,233[[5]](#cite_note-census-5) (as of 2021), Cape Verde is [among the least populous countries in Africa](/wiki/List_of_African_countries_by_population "List of African countries by population"). The Cape Verdean people trace their ancestry primarily to West African populations, with additional contributions from early Portuguese settlers and other groups who came to the islands during the Atlantic era. A sizeable [diaspora](/wiki/Cape_Verdean_diaspora "Cape Verdean diaspora") exists across the world, especially in the United States and Portugal, considerably outnumbering the inhabitants on the islands. Cape Verde is a member state of the [African Union](/wiki/African_Union "African Union").

The official language is [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"),[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) while the recognized national language is [Cape Verdean Creole](/wiki/Cape_Verdean_Creole "Cape Verdean Creole") (Crioulo), which is spoken by the vast majority of the population. As of the 2021 census, the most populous islands were [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago,_Cape_Verde "Santiago, Cape Verde") (269,370)—which hosts the country's capital and largest city, [Praia](/wiki/Praia "Praia")—[São Vicente](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Vicente,_Cape_Verde "São Vicente, Cape Verde") (74,016), [Santo Antão](/wiki/Santo_Ant%C3%A3o,_Cape_Verde "Santo Antão, Cape Verde") (36,632), [Fogo](/wiki/Fogo,_Cape_Verde "Fogo, Cape Verde") (33,519) and [Sal](/wiki/Sal,_Cape_Verde "Sal, Cape Verde") (33,347). The largest cities are Praia (137,868), [Mindelo](/wiki/Mindelo "Mindelo") (69,013), [Espargos](/wiki/Espargos "Espargos") (24,500) and [Assomada](/wiki/Assomada "Assomada") (21,297).[[11]](#cite_note-15)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The country is named after the [Cap-Vert](/wiki/Cap-Vert "Cap-Vert") peninsula on the [Senegalese](/wiki/Senegal "Senegal") coast.[[12]](#cite_note-16) The name *Cap-Vert*, in turn, comes from the [Portuguese language](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language") *Cabo Verde* ('green cape'), the name given to it by Portuguese explorers in 1444, a few years before they came across the islands. Historically, the name has been anglicized as Cape Verde. In 2013, the country's delegation informed the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") that only *Cabo Verde* and no other translations should be used for official purposes.[[9]](#cite_note-NameNatGeo-13)[[13]](#cite_note-17)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Cape Verde](/wiki/History_of_Cape_Verde "History of Cape Verde")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cc/Insulae_Capitis_viridis-1598.jpg/330px-Insulae_Capitis_viridis-1598.jpg)](/wiki/File:Insulae_Capitis_viridis-1598.jpg)

*Insulae Capitis Viridis* (1598), showing Cape Verde

The archipelago was formed approximately 40–50 million years ago during the [Eocene](/wiki/Eocene "Eocene") epoch. Before the arrival of Europeans, the Cape Verde Islands were uninhabited.[[14]](#cite_note-18)[[15]](#cite_note-19)[[16]](#cite_note-20) They were discovered by [Genoese](/wiki/Republic_of_Genoa "Republic of Genoa") and Portuguese navigators around 1456. According to Portuguese official records,[[17]](#cite_note-21) the first discoveries were made by Genoa-born [António de Noli](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_de_Noli "António de Noli"), who was afterwards appointed governor of Cape Verde by Portuguese King [Afonso V](/wiki/Afonso_V_of_Portugal "Afonso V of Portugal"). Other navigators mentioned as contributing to discoveries on the Cape Verde archipelago are [Diogo Dias](/wiki/Diogo_Dias "Diogo Dias"), Diogo Afonso, [Venetian](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice") [Alvise Cadamosto](/wiki/Alvise_Cadamosto "Alvise Cadamosto") and [Diogo Gomes](/wiki/Diogo_Gomes "Diogo Gomes") (who had accompanied António de Noli on his voyage of discovery, and who claimed to have been the first to land on [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago,_Cape_Verde "Santiago, Cape Verde") and the first to name that island).

In 1462, Portuguese settlers arrived at Santiago and founded a settlement they called Ribeira Grande. Today it is called [Cidade Velha](/wiki/Cidade_Velha "Cidade Velha") ("Old City"), to distinguish it from [Ribeira Grande](/wiki/Ribeira_Grande,_Cape_Verde "Ribeira Grande, Cape Verde"). The original Ribeira Grande was the first permanent European settlement in the [tropics](/wiki/Tropics "Tropics").[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22)

In the 16th century, the archipelago prospered from the [Atlantic slave trade](/wiki/Atlantic_slave_trade "Atlantic slave trade").[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22) Pirates occasionally attacked the Portuguese settlements. [Francis Drake](/wiki/Francis_Drake "Francis Drake"), an English [privateer](/wiki/Privateer "Privateer"), twice sacked Ribeira Grande in 1585 when it was a part of the [Iberian Union](/wiki/Iberian_Union "Iberian Union").[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22) After a French attack in 1712, the town declined in importance relative to nearby [Praia](/wiki/Praia "Praia"), which became the capital in 1770.[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22)

The decline in the slave trade in the 19th century resulted in an economic crisis. Cape Verde's early prosperity slowly vanished. However, the islands' position astride mid-Atlantic shipping lanes made Cape Verde an ideal location for re-supplying ships. Because of its excellent harbour [Mindelo](/wiki/Mindelo "Mindelo"), located on the island of [São Vicente](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Vicente,_Cape_Verde "São Vicente, Cape Verde"), became an important commercial centre.[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22) Diplomat [Edmund Roberts](/wiki/Edmund_Roberts_(diplomat) "Edmund Roberts (diplomat)") visited Cape Verde in 1832.[[19]](#cite_note-Roberts-23) Cape Verde was the first stop of [Charles Darwin](/wiki/Charles_Darwin "Charles Darwin")'s voyage with [HMS *Beagle*](/wiki/HMS_Beagle "HMS Beagle") in 1832.[[20]](#cite_note-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Garthpool_SLV_AllanGreen.jpg/330px-Garthpool_SLV_AllanGreen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Garthpool_SLV_AllanGreen.jpg)

The Scottish-built grain-ship *[Garthpool](/wiki/Garthpool_(barque) "Garthpool (barque)")*, wrecked at [Boa Vista](/wiki/Boa_Vista,_Cape_Verde "Boa Vista, Cape Verde"), in 1928

With few natural resources and inadequate sustainable investment from the Portuguese, the citizens grew increasingly discontented with the colonial masters, who refused to provide the local authorities with more autonomy. In 1951, Portugal changed Cape Verde's status from a colony to an overseas province in an attempt to blunt growing nationalism.[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22)

In 1956, [Amílcar Cabral](/wiki/Am%C3%ADlcar_Cabral "Amílcar Cabral") and a group of fellow Cape Verdeans and Guineans organized (in [Portuguese Guinea](/wiki/Portuguese_Guinea "Portuguese Guinea")) the clandestine [African Party for the Independence of Guinea and Cape Verde](/wiki/African_Party_for_the_Independence_of_Guinea_and_Cape_Verde "African Party for the Independence of Guinea and Cape Verde") (PAIGC).[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22) It demanded improvement in economic, social, and political conditions in Cape Verde and Portuguese Guinea and formed the basis of the two nations' independence movement. Moving its headquarters to [Conakry](/wiki/Conakry "Conakry"), Guinea, in 1960, the PAIGC began an armed rebellion against Portugal in 1961. Acts of sabotage eventually grew into a [war in Portuguese Guinea](/wiki/Guinea-Bissau_War_of_Independence "Guinea-Bissau War of Independence") that pitted 10,000 [Soviet Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc")-supported PAIGC soldiers against 35,000 Portuguese and African troops.[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22)

By 1972, the PAIGC controlled much of Portuguese Guinea despite the presence of the Portuguese troops, but the organization did not attempt to disrupt Portuguese control in Cape Verde. Portuguese Guinea declared independence in 1973 and was granted *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* independence in 1974. A budding independence movement—originally led by [Amílcar Cabral](/wiki/Am%C3%ADlcar_Cabral "Amílcar Cabral") who was assassinated in 1973—passed on to his half-brother [Luís Cabral](/wiki/Lu%C3%ADs_Cabral "Luís Cabral") and culminated in independence for the archipelago in 1975.

### Independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Independence")]

Following the [April 1974 revolution in Portugal](/wiki/Carnation_Revolution "Carnation Revolution"), the PAIGC became an active political movement in Cape Verde. In December 1974, the PAIGC and Portugal signed an agreement providing for a transitional government composed of Portuguese and Cape Verdeans. On 30 June 1975, Cape Verdeans elected a National Assembly which received the instruments of independence from Portugal on 5 July 1975.[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22) The PAIGC ruled Cape Verde as a one-party state until 1990.

On 2 February 2024, Cape Verde became the third African country to be free of [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria").[[21]](#cite_note-25)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

### Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Government")]

Cape Verde is a stable [semi-presidential](/wiki/Semi-presidential_system "Semi-presidential system") [representative democratic](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy") [republic](/wiki/Republic "Republic").[[4]](#cite_note-SpL-4)[[22]](#cite_note-constitution-26) In 2020 it was the most [democratic nation in Africa](/wiki/Democracy_in_Africa "Democracy in Africa"), ranking 2023 as 45th in the world, according to the electoral democracy score of the [V-Dem Democracy indices](/wiki/V-Dem_Democracy_indices "V-Dem Democracy indices").[[23]](#cite_note-vdem_dataset-27)

The constitution – adopted in 1980 and revised in 1992, 1995 and 1999 – defines the basic principles of its government. The [president](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_Cape_Verde "List of presidents of Cape Verde") is the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and is elected by [popular vote](/wiki/Direct_election "Direct election") for a five-year term.[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22) The [prime minister](/wiki/List_of_prime_ministers_of_Cape_Verde "List of prime ministers of Cape Verde") is the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") and proposes other ministers and secretaries of state. The prime minister is nominated by the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_(Cape_Verde) "National Assembly (Cape Verde)") and appointed by the president.[[24]](#cite_note-28) Members of the National Assembly are elected by popular vote for five-year terms. In 2016, three parties held seats in the National Assembly – MpD (36), PAICV (25), and the Cape Verdean Independent Democratic Union (UCID) (3).[[25]](#cite_note-yahoo.com-29) The two main political parties are PAICV and MpD.[[25]](#cite_note-yahoo.com-29)

The judicial system consists of a Supreme Court of Justice – whose members are appointed by the president, the National Assembly, and the Board of the Judiciary – and regional courts. Separate courts hear civil, constitutional, and criminal cases. Appeals are to the Supreme Court.[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22)

### Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

Further information: [Foreign relations of Cape Verde](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Cape_Verde "Foreign relations of Cape Verde")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Cape_Verdean_embassies.PNG/330px-Cape_Verdean_embassies.PNG)](/wiki/File:Cape_Verdean_embassies.PNG)

Map of countries with Cape Verdean embassies

Cape Verde follows a policy of [nonalignment](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement") and seeks cooperative relations with all friendly states.[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22) Angola, Brazil, China, Libya, Cuba, France, Guinea-Bissau, Germany, Italy, Portugal, Spain, São Tomé and Príncipe, Senegal, Russia, Luxembourg, and the United States maintain embassies in Praia.[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22) Cape Verde maintains a vigorously active foreign policy especially in Africa.[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22)

Cape Verde is a founding member state of the [Community of Portuguese Language Countries](/wiki/Community_of_Portuguese_Language_Countries "Community of Portuguese Language Countries"), also known as the Lusophone Commonwealth, an international organization and political association of [Lusophone](/wiki/Lusophone "Lusophone") nations across four continents, where Portuguese is an official language. Cape Verde has [bilateral relations](/wiki/Bilateralism "Bilateralism") with some Lusophone nations and holds membership in a number of international organizations.[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22) It also participates in most international conferences on economic and political issues.[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22) Since 2007, Cape Verde has a *special partnership* status[[33]](#cite_note-37) with the [EU](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), under the [Cotonou Agreement](/wiki/Cotonou_Agreement "Cotonou Agreement"), and might apply for [special membership](/wiki/Cape_Verde%E2%80%93European_Union_relations#Membership "Cape Verde–European Union relations"), in particular because the [Cape Verdean escudo](/wiki/Cape_Verdean_escudo "Cape Verdean escudo"), the country's currency, is indexed to the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro").[[34]](#cite_note-38) In 2011 Cape Verde ratified the [Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court](/wiki/Rome_Statute_of_the_International_Criminal_Court "Rome Statute of the International Criminal Court").[[35]](#cite_note-39) In 2017 Cape Verde signed the UN treaty on the [Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Prohibition_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons").[[36]](#cite_note-40)

In November 2021, Cape Verde opened its first embassy in Nigeria.[[37]](#cite_note-41)

### Military

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Military")]

Main article: [Cape Verdean Armed Forces](/wiki/Cape_Verdean_Armed_Forces "Cape Verdean Armed Forces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Marins_du_Cap-Vert_en_2011.jpg/250px-Marins_du_Cap-Vert_en_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marins_du_Cap-Vert_en_2011.jpg)

Marines of the Cape Verdean Coast Guard

The military of Cape Verde consists of the National Guard and the Coast Guard; 0.7% of the country's GDP was spent on the military in 2005. Having fought their only battles in the war for independence against Portugal between 1974 and 1975, the efforts of the Cape Verdean armed forces have turned to combatting international drug trafficking. In 2007, together with the [Cape Verdean Police](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Cape_Verde "Law enforcement in Cape Verde"), they carried out Operation Flying Launch (*Operacão Lancha Voadora*), a successful operation to put an end to a drug trafficking group which smuggled cocaine from Colombia to the Netherlands and Germany using the country as a reorder point. The operation took more than three years, being a secret operation during the first two years, and ended in 2010. In 2016, Cape Verdean Armed Forces were involved in the [Monte Tchota massacre](/wiki/Monte_Tchota_massacre "Monte Tchota massacre"), a green-on-green incident that resulted in 11 deaths.[[38]](#cite_note-42)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Geography")]

Main article: [Geography of Cape Verde](/wiki/Geography_of_Cape_Verde "Geography of Cape Verde")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Topographic_map_of_Cape_Verde-en.svg/250px-Topographic_map_of_Cape_Verde-en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Topographic_map_of_Cape_Verde-en.svg)

A topographic map of Cape Verde

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Cape_Verde.2010-11-23.250m-per-px.jpg/250px-Cape_Verde.2010-11-23.250m-per-px.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cape_Verde.2010-11-23.250m-per-px.jpg)

A satellite photo of the Cape Verde islands, 2010

The Cape Verde archipelago is in the Atlantic Ocean, approximately 570 kilometres (350 mi) off the western coast of the African continent, near [Senegal](/wiki/Senegal "Senegal"), [The Gambia](/wiki/The_Gambia "The Gambia"), and [Mauritania](/wiki/Mauritania "Mauritania") as well as part of the [Macaronesia](/wiki/Macaronesia "Macaronesia") [ecoregion](/wiki/Ecoregion "Ecoregion"). It lies between latitudes [14°](/wiki/14th_parallel_north "14th parallel north") and [18°N](/wiki/18th_parallel_north "18th parallel north"), and longitudes [22°](/wiki/22nd_meridian_west "22nd meridian west") and [26°W](/wiki/26th_meridian_west "26th meridian west"). The country is a horseshoe-shaped cluster of ten islands (nine inhabited) and eight islets,[[39]](#cite_note-pc-43) that constitute an area of 4033 km2 (1557 sq mi).[[39]](#cite_note-pc-43)

The islands are spatially divided into two groups:

* The [Barlavento Islands](/wiki/Barlavento_Islands "Barlavento Islands") ([windward](/wiki/Windward_and_leeward "Windward and leeward") islands): [Santo Antão](/wiki/Santo_Ant%C3%A3o,_Cape_Verde "Santo Antão, Cape Verde"), [São Vicente](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Vicente,_Cape_Verde "São Vicente, Cape Verde"), [Santa Luzia](/wiki/Santa_Luzia,_Cape_Verde "Santa Luzia, Cape Verde"), [São Nicolau](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Nicolau,_Cape_Verde "São Nicolau, Cape Verde"), [Sal](/wiki/Sal,_Cape_Verde "Sal, Cape Verde"), [Boa Vista](/wiki/Boa_Vista,_Cape_Verde "Boa Vista, Cape Verde");[[39]](#cite_note-pc-43) and
* The [Sotavento Islands](/wiki/Sotavento_Islands "Sotavento Islands") ([leeward](/wiki/Windward_and_leeward "Windward and leeward")): [Maio](/wiki/Maio,_Cape_Verde "Maio, Cape Verde"), [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago,_Cape_Verde "Santiago, Cape Verde"), [Fogo](/wiki/Fogo,_Cape_Verde "Fogo, Cape Verde"), [Brava](/wiki/Brava,_Cape_Verde "Brava, Cape Verde").[[39]](#cite_note-pc-43)

The largest island, both in size and population, is Santiago, which hosts the nation's capital, Praia, the principal urban agglomeration in the archipelago.[[39]](#cite_note-pc-43) Sal, Boa Vista, and Maio, are fairly flat, sandy, and dry; the other islands are generally rockier with more vegetation.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Calhau.jpg/250px-Calhau.jpg)](/wiki/File:Calhau.jpg)

The beach of Calhau, with Monte Verde in the background, on [São Vicente](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Vicente,_Cape_Verde "São Vicente, Cape Verde")

### Physical geography and geology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Physical geography and geology")]

Main article: [Geology of Cape Verde](/wiki/Geology_of_Cape_Verde "Geology of Cape Verde")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Estr._Ba%C3%ADa_das_Gatas_-_Calhau%2C_Cape_Verde_-_panoramio_%281%29.jpg/250px-Estr._Ba%C3%ADa_das_Gatas_-_Calhau%2C_Cape_Verde_-_panoramio_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Estr._Ba%C3%ADa_das_Gatas_-_Calhau,_Cape_Verde_-_panoramio_(1).jpg)

The countryside in Estrada Baía das Gatas

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Boa_Vista_IMG_3886_25.jpg/250px-Boa_Vista_IMG_3886_25.jpg)](/wiki/File:Boa_Vista_IMG_3886_25.jpg)

Beach east of [Curral Velho](/wiki/Curral_Velho,_Cape_Verde "Curral Velho, Cape Verde"), [Boa Vista](/wiki/Boa_Vista,_Cape_Verde "Boa Vista, Cape Verde")

Geologically, the islands are principally composed of [igneous rocks](/wiki/Igneous_rock "Igneous rock"), with volcanic structures and [pyroclastic](/wiki/Pyroclastic_rock "Pyroclastic rock") debris comprising the majority of the archipelago's total volume. The [volcanic](/wiki/Volcanic_rock "Volcanic rock") and [plutonic rocks](/wiki/Plutonic_rock "Plutonic rock") are distinctly basic; the archipelago is a soda-alkaline [petrographic](/wiki/Petrography "Petrography") province, with a [petrologic](/wiki/Petrology "Petrology") succession similar to that found in other Macaronesian islands. The islands lie on a bathymetric swell known as the [Cape Verde Rise](/wiki/Cape_Verde_Rise "Cape Verde Rise").[[40]](#cite_note-44) The rise is one of the largest protuberances in the world's oceans, rising 2.2 kilometres (1.4 miles) in a semi-circular region of 1200 km2 (460 sq mi), associated with a rise of the [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid").[[41]](#cite_note-Pim_et_al.,_2008,_p.422-45)

Magnetic anomalies identified in the vicinity of the archipelago indicate that the structures forming the islands date back 125–150 million years: the islands date from 8 million (in the west) to 20 million years (in the east).[[41]](#cite_note-Pim_et_al.,_2008,_p.422-45) The oldest exposed rocks occurred on Maio and the northern peninsula of Santiago and are 128–131 million-year-old [pillow lavas](/wiki/Pillow_lava "Pillow lava"). The first stage of volcanism in the islands began in the early [Miocene](/wiki/Miocene "Miocene") and reached its peak at the end of this period when the islands reached their maximum sizes.

Historical volcanism (within human settlement) has been restricted to Fogo.[[42]](#cite_note-46) [Pico do Fogo](/wiki/Pico_do_Fogo "Pico do Fogo"), the largest active volcano in the region, erupted in 2014. It has an eight-kilometre-diameter (five-mile) [caldera](/wiki/Caldera "Caldera"), the rim of which is at an elevation of 1,600 metres (5,249 feet) and an interior cone that rises to 2,829 metres (9,281 feet) above sea level. The caldera resulted from subsidence, following the partial evacuation (eruption) of the magma chamber, along a cylindrical column from within the magma chamber (at a depth of 8 kilometres (5 miles)).

Extensive [salt flats](/wiki/Salt_pan_(geology) "Salt pan (geology)") are found on Sal and Maio.[[39]](#cite_note-pc-43) On Santiago, Santo Antão, and São Nicolau, arid slopes give way in places to sugarcane fields or banana plantations spread along the base of towering mountains.[[39]](#cite_note-pc-43) Ocean cliffs have been formed by catastrophic [debris landslides](/wiki/Debris_flow "Debris flow").[[43]](#cite_note-47)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Climate")]

Further information: [Geography of Cape Verde § Climate](/wiki/Geography_of_Cape_Verde#Climate "Geography of Cape Verde")

Cape Verde's climate is milder than that of the African mainland because the surrounding sea moderates temperatures, and cold Atlantic currents produce an arid atmosphere. Conversely, the islands do not receive the [upwelling](/wiki/Upwelling "Upwelling") (cold streams) that affect the West African coast, so the air temperature is cooler than in Senegal, but the sea is warmer. Due to the relief of some islands, such as Santiago with its steep mountains, the islands can have [orographically](/wiki/Orographic_lift "Orographic lift") induced precipitation, allowing rich woods and luxuriant vegetation to grow where the humid air condenses, soaking the plants, rocks, soil, logs, moss, etc. On the higher islands and somewhat wetter islands, the climate is suitable for the development of dry monsoon forests and [laurel forests](/wiki/Laurel_forest "Laurel forest").[[39]](#cite_note-pc-43) Cape Verde lies in the [Cape Verde Islands dry forests](/wiki/Cape_Verde_Islands_dry_forests "Cape Verde Islands dry forests") ecoregion.[[44]](#cite_note-DinersteinOlson2017-48) Average temperatures range from 22 °C (72 °F) in February to 27 °C (80.6 °F) in September.[[45]](#cite_note-inmg-49) Cape Verde is part of the [Sahelian](/wiki/Sahel "Sahel") semi-arid belt, with nothing like the rainfall levels of nearby [West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa").[[39]](#cite_note-pc-43) It rains irregularly between August and October, with frequent brief heavy downpours.[[39]](#cite_note-pc-43) A desert is usually defined as terrain that receives less than 250 mm (9.8 in) of annual rainfall. Sal's total of 145 mm (5.7 in) confirms this classification. Most of the year's rain falls in September.[[45]](#cite_note-inmg-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/Ribeira_Principal_%281%29.jpg/250px-Ribeira_Principal_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ribeira_Principal_(1).jpg)

The small valley (or dale) of Principal, [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago,_Cape_Verde "Santiago, Cape Verde")

Because of the infrequent occurrence of rainfall where not mountainous, the landscape is so arid that less than two percent of it is arable.[[46]](#cite_note-50) The archipelago can be divided into four broad ecological zones – arid, semiarid, sub-humid and humid, according to altitude and average annual rainfall ranging from less than 100 millimetres (3.9 inches) in the arid areas of the coast as in the [Deserto de Viana](/wiki/Deserto_de_Viana "Deserto de Viana") (67 millimetres (2.6 inches) in [Sal Rei](/wiki/Sal_Rei "Sal Rei")) to more than 1,000 millimetres (39 inches) in the humid mountain. Most rainfall precipitation is due to condensation of the ocean mist. In some islands, like Santiago, the wetter climate of the interior and the eastern coast contrasts with the drier one on the south/southwest coast.

Western Hemisphere-bound hurricanes often have their early beginnings near the Cape Verde Islands. These [Cape Verde hurricanes](/wiki/Cape_Verde_hurricane "Cape Verde hurricane") can become very intense as they cross warm Atlantic waters. The average hurricane season has about two Cape Verde-type hurricanes, which are usually the largest and most intense storms of the season because they often have plenty of warm open ocean over which to develop before encountering land. The five largest Atlantic tropical cyclones on record have been Cape Verde-type hurricanes. Most of the longest-lived tropical cyclones in the Atlantic basin are Cape Verde hurricanes.[[47]](#cite_note-51)

Since 1851 the islands have been struck by hurricanes twice in recorded history: in 1892 and in 2015 ([Hurricane Fred](/wiki/Hurricane_Fred_(2015) "Hurricane Fred (2015)"), the easternmost hurricane ever to form in the Atlantic).[[48]](#cite_note-52)

According to the president of [Nauru](/wiki/Nauru "Nauru"), in 2011, Cape Verde was ranked the eighth most endangered nation due to flooding from climate change.[[52]](#cite_note-56) In 2023, [UN Secretary-General](/wiki/Secretary-General_of_the_United_Nations "Secretary-General of the United Nations") [António Guterres](/wiki/Ant%C3%B3nio_Guterres "António Guterres") arrived in Cabo Verde to raise concerns about climate change. He said that the country is on the frontlines of the existential crisis generated by climate disruptions and that world leaders need to take action to address the climate crisis.[[53]](#cite_note-57) Cabo Verde is a leader in [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") in sub-Saharan Africa. Currently, 20% of its energy comes from renewable sources, and the goal is to increase that to 50% by 2030.[[54]](#cite_note-58) In 2023, Portugal signed an agreement to forgive €140 million of Cape Verde's debt in exchange for the country investing in environmental projects. This agreement is one of the first [debt-for-nature swaps](/wiki/Debt-for-nature_swap "Debt-for-nature swap") in Africa.[[55]](#cite_note-59)

### Biodiversity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Biodiversity")]

Main articles: [Wildlife of Cape Verde](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Cape_Verde "Wildlife of Cape Verde"), [Flora of Cape Verde](/wiki/Flora_of_Cape_Verde "Flora of Cape Verde"), and [Cape Verde Islands dry forests](/wiki/Cape_Verde_Islands_dry_forests "Cape Verde Islands dry forests")

Cape Verde's isolation has resulted in the islands having several endemic species, particularly birds and reptiles, many of which are endangered by human development. Endemic birds include [Alexander's swift](/wiki/Alexander%27s_swift "Alexander's swift") (*Apus alexandri*), [Bourne's heron](/wiki/Bourne%27s_heron "Bourne's heron") (*Ardea purpurea bournei*), the [Raso lark](/wiki/Raso_lark "Raso lark") (*Alauda razae*), the [Cape Verde warbler](/wiki/Cape_Verde_warbler "Cape Verde warbler") (*Acrocephalus brevipennis*), and the [Iago sparrow](/wiki/Iago_sparrow "Iago sparrow") (*Passer iagoensis*).[[56]](#cite_note-60) The islands are also an important breeding area for seabirds including the [Cape Verde shearwater](/wiki/Cape_Verde_shearwater "Cape Verde shearwater"). Reptiles include the Cape Verde giant gecko (*[Tarentola gigas](/wiki/Tarentola_gigas "Tarentola gigas")*).

[Forest cover](/wiki/Forest_cover "Forest cover") is around 11% of the total land area, equivalent to 45,720 hectares (ha) of forest in 2020, up from 15,380 ha in 1990. In 2020, naturally regenerating forest covered 13,680 ha and planted forest covered 32,040 ha. For the year 2015, 100% of the forest area was reported to be under public ownership.[[57]](#cite_note-61)[[58]](#cite_note-62)

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main article: [Economy of Cape Verde](/wiki/Economy_of_Cape_Verde "Economy of Cape Verde")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Cabo_Verde_Airlines%2C_D4-CCF%2C_Boeing_757-236_%2849587833096%29.jpg/250px-Cabo_Verde_Airlines%2C_D4-CCF%2C_Boeing_757-236_%2849587833096%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cabo_Verde_Airlines,_D4-CCF,_Boeing_757-236_(49587833096).jpg)

Cape Verdean national [flag carrier](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier") [Cabo Verde Airlines](/wiki/Cabo_Verde_Airlines "Cabo Verde Airlines"), formerly known as TACV

Cape Verde's notable economic growth and improvement in living conditions despite a lack of natural resources have garnered international recognition, with other countries and international organisations often providing development aid. Since 2007, the UN has classified it as a [developing nation](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country") rather than a [least developed country](/wiki/Least_developed_countries "Least developed countries").

Cape Verde has few [natural resources](/wiki/Natural_resource "Natural resource"). Only five of the ten main islands (Santiago, Santo Antão, São Nicolau, Fogo, and Brava) normally support significant agricultural production,[[61]](#cite_note-65) and over 90% of all food consumed is imported. Mineral resources include salt, [pozzolana](/wiki/Pozzolana "Pozzolana") (a volcanic rock used in cement production), and [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone").[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22) Its small number of wineries making Portuguese-style wines have traditionally focused on the domestic market, but have recently met with some international acclaim.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The economy is service-oriented, with commerce, transport, and public services accounting for more than 70% of the GDP.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Although nearly 35% of the population lives in rural areas, agriculture and fishing contribute only about 9% of GDP. Light manufacturing accounts for most of the remainder. Fish and shellfish are plentiful, and small quantities are exported. Cape Verde has cold storage and freezing facilities and [fish processing](/wiki/Fish_processing "Fish processing") plants in Mindelo, Praia, and on Sal. Expatriate Cape Verdeans contribute an amount estimated at 20% of GDP to the domestic economy through [remittances](/wiki/Remittance "Remittance").[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22)
Despite having few natural resources and being semi-desert, the country has the highest living standards in the region and has attracted thousands of immigrants of different nationalities.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Since 1991, the government has pursued market-oriented economic policies, including an open welcome to foreign investors and a far-reaching privatization programme. It established as top development priorities the promotion of a market economy and the private sector; the development of tourism, light manufacturing industries, and fisheries; and the development of transport, communications, and energy facilities. From 1994 to 2000 about $407 million in foreign investments were made or planned, of which 58% were in tourism,[[62]](#cite_note-66) 17% in industry, 4% in infrastructure, and 21% in fisheries and services.[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22)

In 2011, a wind farm was built on four islands that supplies about 30% of the electricity of the country.[[63]](#cite_note-67)
As host to the [ECOWAS](/wiki/ECOWAS "ECOWAS") Regional Centre for Renewable Energy and Energy Efficiency, inaugurated in 2010, Cape Verde had planned to lead by example by becoming entirely reliant on renewable energy sources by 2025.[[64]](#cite_note-UNESCO_Science_Report_2021-68)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] This policy was consistent with the host of documents adopted in 2015 paving the way to more sustainable development, including Cape Verde's *Transformational Agenda to 2030*, its *National Renewable Energy Plan* and its *Low Carbon and Climate-resilient Development Strategy*. Two years later, these were followed by a *Strategic Plan for Sustainable Development, 2017–2021*.[[64]](#cite_note-UNESCO_Science_Report_2021-68) Since then, Cape Verde has pushed back this goal, aiming for 50% of energy produced by renewable sources by 2030 and 100% by 2050.[[65]](#cite_note-69)

Between 2000 and 2009, real GDP increased on average by over 7% a year, well above the average for sub-Saharan countries and faster than most small island economies in the region. Strong economic performance was bolstered by one of the fastest-growing tourism industries in the world, as well as by substantial capital inflows that allowed Cape Verde to build up national currency reserves to the current 3.5 months of imports. Unemployment has been falling rapidly, and the country is on track to achieve most of the UN Millennium Development Goals – including halving its 1990 poverty level. In 2007, Cape Verde joined the [World Trade Organization](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization") (WTO) and in 2008 the country graduated from Least Developed Country (LDC) to Middle Income Country (MIC) status.[[66]](#cite_note-70)[[67]](#cite_note-71)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/CV-praia-in_der_avenida_cabral.jpg/250px-CV-praia-in_der_avenida_cabral.jpg)](/wiki/File:CV-praia-in_der_avenida_cabral.jpg)

Cabral Avenue, one of the main symbols of Cape Verde's development

Cape Verde has significant cooperation with Portugal at every level of the economy, which has led it to link its currency first to the [Portuguese escudo](/wiki/Portuguese_escudo "Portuguese escudo") and, in 1999, to the euro. On 23 June 2008 Cape Verde became the 153rd member of the WTO.[[68]](#cite_note-72) In early January 2018, the government announced that the minimum wage would be raised to 13,000 CVE (€118) per month, from 11,000 CVE, which was effective in mid-January 2018.[[69]](#cite_note-73)[[70]](#cite_note-74)

The [European Commission](/wiki/European_Commission "European Commission")'s total allocation for the period of 2008–2013 foreseen for Cape Verde to address "poverty reduction, in particular in rural and peri-urban areas where women are heading the households, as well as good governance" amounts to €54.1 million.[[71]](#cite_note-75)

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Tourism")]

Main article: [Tourism in Cape Verde](/wiki/Tourism_in_Cape_Verde "Tourism in Cape Verde")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Mindelo_portogrande.jpg/250px-Mindelo_portogrande.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mindelo_portogrande.jpg)

Yachts in Porto Grande, Mindelo, on the island of [São Vicente](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Vicente,_Cape_Verde "São Vicente, Cape Verde"). Tourism is a growing source of income on the islands.

Cape Verde's strategic location at the crossroads of mid-Atlantic air and sea lanes has been enhanced by significant improvements at Mindelo's harbour ([Porto Grande](/wiki/Porto_Grande_Bay "Porto Grande Bay")) and at [Sal's](/wiki/Am%C3%ADlcar_Cabral_International_Airport "Amílcar Cabral International Airport") and [Praia's international airports](/wiki/Nelson_Mandela_International_Airport "Nelson Mandela International Airport"). [Aristides Pereira International Airport](/wiki/Aristides_Pereira_International_Airport "Aristides Pereira International Airport") was opened in 2007, and [Cesária Évora Airport](/wiki/Ces%C3%A1ria_%C3%89vora_Airport "Cesária Évora Airport") was opened in 2009. Ship repair facilities at Mindelo were opened in 1983.[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22)

The major ports are Mindelo and Praia, but all other islands have smaller port facilities. In addition to the international airport on Sal, [airports](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Cape_Verde "List of airports in Cape Verde") have been built on all of the inhabited islands. All but the airports on Brava and Santo Antão enjoy scheduled air service. The archipelago has 3,050 km (1,895 mi) of roads, of which 1,010 km (628 mi) are paved, most using [cobblestone](/wiki/Cobblestone "Cobblestone").[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Main articles: [Cape Verdeans](/wiki/Cape_Verdeans "Cape Verdeans") and [Demographics of Cape Verde](/wiki/Demographics_of_Cape_Verde "Demographics of Cape Verde")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Cape-Verde-demography.png/250px-Cape-Verde-demography.png)](/wiki/File:Cape-Verde-demography.png)

Cape Verde's population, 1961–2003

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Pyramide_Cap-Vert.PNG/250px-Pyramide_Cap-Vert.PNG)](/wiki/File:Pyramide_Cap-Vert.PNG)

Cape Verde's [population pyramid](/wiki/Population_pyramid "Population pyramid"), 2005

Cape Verde had a population of 491,233 in 2021.[[5]](#cite_note-census-5) A large proportion (236,000) of Cape Verdeans live on Santiago.[[72]](#cite_note-cvp-76) Cape Verdeans are descendants of Africans (free or enslaved) and Europeans of various origins. There are also Cape Verdeans who have Jewish ancestors from North Africa, mainly on Boa Vista, Santiago, and Santo Antão.

The country's historical trajectory included, from the beginning, a process of social class formation. At this moment, the absence of a "bourgeoisie" can be seen, but the existence of several types of "petty bourgeoisie" is numerically significant. The majority of the population is, however, made up of the peasantry and some working class.[[73]](#cite_note-77)

### Languages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Languages")]

The official language is [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language").[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) It is the language of instruction and government. It is also used in newspapers, television, and radio. [Cape Verdean Creole](/wiki/Cape_Verdean_Creole "Cape Verdean Creole") (*Kriolu*) is used colloquially and is the mother tongue of virtually all Cape Verdeans. The national constitution calls for measures to give it parity with Portuguese.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) There is a substantial body of literature in Creole, especially in the [Santiago Creole](/wiki/Santiago_Creole "Santiago Creole") and the [São Vicente Creole](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Vicente_Creole "São Vicente Creole"). *Kriolu* has been gaining prestige since the nation's independence from Portugal.

The differences between the forms of the language within the islands have been a major obstacle to the standardization of the language. Some people have advocated the development of two standards: a north (Barlavento) standard, centred on the São Vicente Creole, and a south (Sotavento) standard, centred on the Santiago Creole. [Manuel Veiga](/wiki/Manuel_Veiga_(writer) "Manuel Veiga (writer)"), a linguist and minister of culture of Cape Verde, is the premier proponent of *Kriolu*'s officialization and standardization.[[74]](#cite_note-78)

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Religion")]

Further information: [Religion in Cape Verde](/wiki/Religion_in_Cape_Verde "Religion in Cape Verde")

Religion in Cape Verde (2010)[[75]](#cite_note-Pew_Forum_—_Religion_in_Cape_Verde-79)

1. [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") (78.7%)
2. Other Christian (10.4%)
3. Other or non-religious (10.9%)

The vast majority of Cape Verdeans are Christian; reflecting centuries of Portuguese rule, [Catholics](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") make up the single largest religious community, at just under 80 percent, as of 2010 (slightly down from 85 percent of the population in 2007).[[76]](#cite_note-rf-80) Most other religious groups are [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant"), with the evangelical [Church of the Nazarene](/wiki/Church_of_the_Nazarene "Church of the Nazarene") forming the second largest community; other sizeable denominations are the [Seventh-day Adventist Church](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church"), the [Assemblies of God](/wiki/Assemblies_of_God "Assemblies of God"), the [Universal Church of the Kingdom of God](/wiki/Universal_Church_of_the_Kingdom_of_God "Universal Church of the Kingdom of God") and [the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints_in_Cape_Verde "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints in Cape Verde").[[76]](#cite_note-rf-80) [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") is the largest minority religion.[[76]](#cite_note-rf-80) [Judaism](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Cape_Verde "History of the Jews in Cape Verde") had a historical presence during the colonial era.[[77]](#cite_note-81) [Atheists](/wiki/Atheists "Atheists") constitute less than 1 percent of the population.[[76]](#cite_note-rf-80) Many Cape Verdeans [syncretize](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism") Christianity with [indigenous African beliefs and customs](/wiki/Traditional_African_religions "Traditional African religions").[[18]](#cite_note-bn-22)

### Emigration and immigration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Emigration and immigration")]

Main article: [Cape Verdean diaspora](/wiki/Cape_Verdean_diaspora "Cape Verdean diaspora")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Minist%C3%A9rio_da_Cultura_-_Claridalia_%2818%29.jpg/250px-Minist%C3%A9rio_da_Cultura_-_Claridalia_%2818%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Minist%C3%A9rio_da_Cultura_-_Claridalia_(18).jpg)

People in [Santiago](/wiki/Santiago,_Cape_Verde "Santiago, Cape Verde"), the largest island in the country

Almost twice as many Cape Verdeans live abroad (nearly one million) than in the country itself.[[78]](#cite_note-82) The islands have a long history of emigration, and Cape Verdeans are highly dispersed worldwide, from [Macau](/wiki/Macau "Macau") to [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti") and [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") to [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden").[[79]](#cite_note-83) The diaspora may be much larger than official statistics indicate, as, until independence in 1975, Cape Verdean immigrants had Portuguese passports. The majority live in the United States and Western Europe, with the former hosting the largest overseas population at 500,000. Most in the U.S. are concentrated in [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England"), particularly [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"), [New Bedford](/wiki/New_Bedford,_Massachusetts "New Bedford, Massachusetts"), and [Providence](/wiki/Providence,_Rhode_Island "Providence, Rhode Island"); [Brockton, Massachusetts](/wiki/Brockton,_Massachusetts "Brockton, Massachusetts"), has the largest community of any American city (18,832).[[80]](#cite_note-84)

Cape Verdeans have been migrating to Massachusetts since the 1840s, but most of the current population arrived in the 1970s.[[81]](#cite_note-:3-85) They are now one of the top ten immigrant groups in Boston and the largest hailing from Africa. The first wave of immigrants came to Massachusetts to work in the [whaling](/wiki/Whaling "Whaling") industry.[[81]](#cite_note-:3-85) When whaling declined, they moved into maritime jobs, seasonal agricultural work (like picking cranberries), and factory work. The second wave of immigrants arrived after Cape Verde gained independence in 1975. They also found work in factories, but as manufacturing plants closed down, they moved into the service industry in the 1990s. Immigrants have developed a vibrant small business sector, including restaurants, groceries, real estate and insurance offices, and other enterprises.[[81]](#cite_note-:3-85) Immigrants in the U.S. have a long history of enlistment in the U.S. military, with a presence in every major conflict from the Revolutionary War to the Vietnam War.[[82]](#cite_note-86)

Due to centuries of colonial ties, the second largest number of Cape Verdeans live in Portugal (150,000), with sizeable communities in the former Portuguese colonies of Angola (45,000) and [São Tomé and Príncipe](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Tom%C3%A9_and_Pr%C3%ADncipe "São Tomé and Príncipe") (25,000). Major populations exist in countries with cultural and linguistic similarities, such as Spain (65,500), France (25,000), Senegal (25,000), and Italy (20,000). Other large communities live in the United Kingdom (35,500), the Netherlands (20,000, of which 15,000 are concentrated in [Rotterdam](/wiki/Rotterdam "Rotterdam")), and Luxembourg and Scandinavia (7,000). Outside the U.S. and Europe, the biggest Cape Verdean populations are in Mexico (5,000) and Argentina (8,000).

Over the years, Cape Verde has increasingly become a net recipient of migrants due to its relatively high per capita income, political and social stability, and civil freedom.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Chinese make up a sizeable and important segment of the foreign population, while nearby West African countries account for most immigration. In the 21st century, a few thousand Europeans and Latin Americans have settled in the country, mostly professionals, entrepreneurs, and retirees. Over 22,000 foreign-born residents are naturalized, hailing from over 90 countries.

### Health

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Health")]

Main article: [Health in Cape Verde](/wiki/Health_in_Cape_Verde "Health in Cape Verde")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/PraiaCentrodeSa%C3%BAde.jpg/330px-PraiaCentrodeSa%C3%BAde.jpg)](/wiki/File:PraiaCentrodeSa%C3%BAde.jpg)

A health clinic in a residential area of Praia

The infant mortality rate among children between 0 and 5 years old is 15 per 1,000 live births according to the latest (2017) data from the National Statistics Bureau,[[83]](#cite_note-ine.cv-87) while the maternal mortality rate is 42 deaths per 100,000 live births. The HIV-AIDS prevalence rate among Cape Verdeans between 15 and 49 years old is 0.8%.[[84]](#cite_note-88)

According to the latest data (2017) from the National Statistics Bureau,[[83]](#cite_note-ine.cv-87) life expectancy at birth is 76.2 years; that is, 72.2 years for males and 80.2 years for females. There are six hospitals: two central hospitals (Praia and Mindelo) and four regional hospitals (in Santa Catarina, São Antão, Fogo, and Sal). In addition, there are 28 health centres, 35 sanitation centres, and a variety of private clinics.

The population is among the healthiest in Africa. Since its independence, it has greatly improved its health indicators. Besides having been promoted to the group of "medium development" countries in 2007, leaving the [least developed countries](/wiki/Least_developed_countries "Least developed countries") category (becoming the second country to do so[[85]](#cite_note-89)), as of 2020 it was the [11th best ranked](/wiki/List_of_African_countries_by_Human_Development_Index "List of African countries by Human Development Index") country in Africa in its [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index"). The total expenditure on health was 7.1% of GDP (2015). Cabo Verde ranks 48th out of 127 countries with sufficient data, with a GHI score of 9.2, indicating low hunger.[[86]](#cite_note-90)

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in Cape Verde](/wiki/Education_in_Cape_Verde "Education in Cape Verde")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/Finalistas_do_jardim_infantil.jpg/330px-Finalistas_do_jardim_infantil.jpg)](/wiki/File:Finalistas_do_jardim_infantil.jpg)

A kindergarten graduation on Santiago Island

Cape Verde has one of the best educational systems in Africa, ranked 8th by the World Education Forum in 2023.[[87]](#cite_note-91) Although the educational system is similar to the Portuguese system, over the years the local universities have been increasingly adopting the American educational system; for instance, all ten existing universities offer four-year bachelor's degree programmes as opposed to five-year bachelor's degree programmes that existed before 2010. Primary school education is mandatory and free for children between ages 6-14.[[88]](#cite_note-ilab-92)

In 2011, the net enrolment ratio for primary school was 85%.[[88]](#cite_note-ilab-92)[[89]](#cite_note-93) Approximately 91% of the total population over 15 years of age is literate,[[90]](#cite_note-94) and roughly 25% of the population holds a college degree;[[91]](#cite_note-95) a significant number of these college graduates hold doctorate degrees in different academic fields. Textbooks have been made available to 90 percent of school children, and 98 percent of the teachers have attended in-service teacher training.[[88]](#cite_note-ilab-92) Although most children have access to education, some problems remain.[[88]](#cite_note-ilab-92) For example, there is insufficient spending on school materials, lunches, and books.[[88]](#cite_note-ilab-92)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Universidade_de_Santiago.jpg/250px-Universidade_de_Santiago.jpg)](/wiki/File:Universidade_de_Santiago.jpg)

University of Santiago

As of October 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit), there were 69 secondary schools (including 19 private secondary schools) and at least 10 universities. In 2015, 23% of the population had either attended or graduated from secondary schools. When it came to higher education, 9% of men and 8% of women held a bachelor's degree or had attended universities. The total expenditure on education was 5.6% of GDP (2010). The mean years of schooling of adults over 25 years is 12. These trends were held in 2017. Cape Verde stands out in West Africa for the quality and inclusiveness of its higher education system. As of 2017, one in four young people attended university and one-third of students opted for fields of science, technology, engineering, and mathematics.[[64]](#cite_note-UNESCO_Science_Report_2021-68) Women made up one-third of students but two-thirds of graduates in 2018.[[64]](#cite_note-UNESCO_Science_Report_2021-68)

### Science and technology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Science and technology")]

Main article: [Science and technology in Cape Verde](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Cape_Verde "Science and technology in Cape Verde")

In 2011, Cape Verde devoted just 0.07% of its GDP to research and development, among the lowest rates in West Africa. The Ministry of Higher Education, Science and Culture plan to strengthen the research and academic sectors by emphasizing greater mobility, through exchange programmes and international cooperation agreements. As part of this strategy, Cape Verde is participating in the Ibero-American academic mobility programme that expects to mobilize 200,000 academics between 2015 and 2020.[[92]](#cite_note-:1-96) Cape Verde was ranked 95th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[93]](#cite_note-97)[[94]](#cite_note-98)

Cape Verde counted 25 researchers in 2011, a researcher density of 51 per million inhabitants. The world average was 1,083 per million in 2013. All 25 researchers were working in the government sector in 2011 and one in three were women (36%). There was no research being conducted in either medical or agricultural sciences. Of the eight engineers involved in research and development, one was a woman. Three of the five researchers working in natural sciences were women, as were three of the six social scientists and two of the five researchers from the humanities.[[92]](#cite_note-:1-96)

In 2015, the government announced a project to build a technology park for business, research, and development. Named TechPark Cabo Verde, operations began in 2023, and the technology park had its official inauguration in 2025[[95]](#cite_note-99). The project is funded by both the [African Development Bank](/wiki/African_Development_Bank "African Development Bank") and the government of Cape Verde. The goal of the endeavour, according to Minister of Finance [Olavo Correia](/wiki/Olavo_Correia "Olavo Correia"), is "to attract large international companies to set up shop [in order] to help local companies and start-ups become more competitive".[[96]](#cite_note-100)

Cape Verde has a high rate of Internet penetration and a growing mobile phone market. The government has invested in improving ICT infrastructure and has created a number of initiatives to promote the development of the digital economy. The digital economy has the potential to create jobs, boost economic growth, and improve the quality of life.[[97]](#cite_note-101)

### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Crime")]

Further information: [Crime in Cape Verde](/wiki/Crime_in_Cape_Verde "Crime in Cape Verde")

Theft and burglary are common, especially in crowded environments such as marketplaces, festivals, and celebrations.[[98]](#cite_note-us-102) Often the perpetrators of these crimes are gangs of street children.[[98]](#cite_note-us-102) Murders are concentrated in the major population centres of Praia and Mindelo.[[98]](#cite_note-us-102)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Ch%C3%A3_das_Caldeiras-Musiciens.jpg/250px-Ch%C3%A3_das_Caldeiras-Musiciens.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ch%C3%A3_das_Caldeiras-Musiciens.jpg)

Cape Verdeans are a very musical people; the [Chã das Caldeiras](/wiki/Ch%C3%A3_das_Caldeiras "Chã das Caldeiras") group is an example.

The [culture of Cape Verde](/wiki/Culture_of_Cape_Verde "Culture of Cape Verde") is characterized by a mixture of African and European elements, while the language and religion are of European origin, several other aspects, such as dance and music, are a unique blend of the cultural heritage of the two different continents.

Football games and church activities are typical sources of social interaction and entertainment.[[39]](#cite_note-pc-43) The traditional walk around the *praça* ([town square](/wiki/Town_square "Town square")) to meet friends is practised regularly in Cape Verdean towns.[[39]](#cite_note-pc-43)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Media")]

Further information: [Media of Cape Verde](/wiki/Media_of_Cape_Verde "Media of Cape Verde")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Newspapers_of_Cape_Verde.jpg/250px-Newspapers_of_Cape_Verde.jpg)](/wiki/File:Newspapers_of_Cape_Verde.jpg)

Newspapers of Cape Verde including *Expresso das Ilhas*, *A Nação* and *Já*

In towns with electricity, television is available on three channels; one state-owned ([RTC](/wiki/RTC_(Cape_Verde) "RTC (Cape Verde)") – TCV) and three foreign-owned: RTI Cabo Verde launched by the Portuguese-based RTI in 2005; [Record Cabo Verde](/wiki/Record_Cabo_Verde "Record Cabo Verde"), launched by the Brazilian-based [Rede Record](/wiki/Rede_Record "Rede Record") on 31 March 2007; and as of 2016, TV CPLP.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Premium channels available include the Cape Verdean versions of Boom TV and Zap Cabo Verde, two channels owned by Brazil's Record.[[99]](#cite_note-103) Other premium channels are available in Cape Verde, especially satellite network channels which are common in hotels and villas, though availability is otherwise limited. One such channel is [RDP África](/wiki/RDP_%C3%81frica "RDP África"), the African version of the Portuguese radio station [RDP](/wiki/Radiodifus%C3%A3o_Portuguesa "Radiodifusão Portuguesa").

As of early 2023, about 99% of the population own an active cellular phone, 70% have access to the Internet, 11% own a landline telephone, and 2% subscribe to local cable TV.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 2004, there were seven radio stations: six independent and one state-owned. The media is operated by the Cape-Verdean News Agency (secondarily as [Inforpress](/wiki/Inforpress "Inforpress")). Nationwide radio stations include RCV, RCV+, Radio Kriola, and the religious station Radio Nova. Local radio stations include Rádio Praia, the first radio station in Cape Verde; Praia FM, the first FM station in the nation; [Rádio Barlavento](/wiki/R%C3%A1dio_Barlavento "Rádio Barlavento"), [Rádio Clube do Mindelo](/wiki/R%C3%A1dio_Clube_do_Mindelo "Rádio Clube do Mindelo") and Radio Morabeza in Mindelo.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Music")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Ces%C3%A1ria_%C3%89vora_%283%29.jpg/250px-Ces%C3%A1ria_%C3%89vora_%283%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ces%C3%A1ria_%C3%89vora_(3).jpg)

[Cesária Évora](/wiki/Ces%C3%A1ria_%C3%89vora "Cesária Évora"), Cape Verdean singer

The Cape Verdean people are known for their musicality, well expressed by popular manifestations such as the Carnaval of Mindelo. [Cape Verdean music](/wiki/Music_of_Cape_Verde "Music of Cape Verde") incorporates "African, Portuguese, and Brazilian influences."[[100]](#cite_note-manuel-104) The national music is the *[morna](/wiki/Morna_(music) "Morna (music)")*, a melancholy and lyrical song form typically sung in Cape Verdean Creole. The most popular music genre after *morna* is the *[coladeira](/wiki/Coladeira "Coladeira")*, followed by *[funaná](/wiki/Funan%C3%A1 "Funaná")* and *[batuque](/wiki/Batuque_(music) "Batuque (music)")* music. [Cesária Évora](/wiki/Ces%C3%A1ria_%C3%89vora "Cesária Évora") was the best-known Cape Verdean singer in the world, known as the "barefoot diva", because she liked to perform barefooted on stage. She was also referred to as "The Queen of Morna"[[101]](#cite_note-105) as opposed to her uncle [Bana](/wiki/Bana_(singer) "Bana (singer)"), who was referred to as "King of Morna". The Cape Verdean diaspora experience is reflected in many artistic and cultural expressions, such as Évora's song *[Sodade](/wiki/Sodade "Sodade")*.[[102]](#cite_note-106) Other singers include [Sara Tavares](/wiki/Sara_Tavares "Sara Tavares"), [Lura](/wiki/Lura_(singer) "Lura (singer)") and [Mayra Andrade](/wiki/Mayra_Andrade "Mayra Andrade").

Another great exponent of traditional music from Cape Verde was [Antonio Vicente Lopes](/wiki/Antoninho_Travadinha "Antoninho Travadinha"), better known as Travadinha, and [Ildo Lobo](/wiki/Ildo_Lobo "Ildo Lobo"), who died in 2004. The House of Culture in the centre of the city of Praia is called Ildo Lobo House of Culture in his honour.

### Dance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Dance")]

Traditional dance is a mix of West and Central African influences. The most popular dance style is called *[funaná](/wiki/Funan%C3%A1 "Funaná")* which originated on Santiago and is danced solo or in pairs with fast hip movements and a lively rhythm. Another popular dance style is *[coladeira](/wiki/Coladeira "Coladeira")*, a slower dance style that originated on Sao Vicente. *[Batuque](/wiki/Batuque_(music_and_dance) "Batuque (music and dance)")* originated on Santiago and involves a lot of hip movement and percussion. [Zouk](/wiki/Zouk "Zouk") and [Kizomba](/wiki/Kizomba "Kizomba") are newer popular dance styles that originated in other countries.

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Literature")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Praia-Funda%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Am%C3%ADlcar_Cabral_%281%29.jpg/250px-Praia-Funda%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Am%C3%ADlcar_Cabral_%281%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Praia-Funda%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Am%C3%ADlcar_Cabral_(1).jpg)

*Fundação Amílcar Cabral*, in Praia

Cape Verdean literature is one of the richest of Lusophone Africa. Poets include Paulino Vieira, Manuel de Novas, Sergio Frusoni, Eugénio Tavares, and B. Léza, and authors include [Baltasar Lopes da Silva](/wiki/Baltasar_Lopes_da_Silva "Baltasar Lopes da Silva"), António Aurélio Gonçalves, [Manuel Lopes](/wiki/Manuel_Lopes_(writer) "Manuel Lopes (writer)"), [Orlanda Amarílis](/wiki/Orlanda_Amar%C3%ADlis "Orlanda Amarílis"), [Henrique Teixeira de Sousa](/wiki/Henrique_Teixeira_de_Sousa "Henrique Teixeira de Sousa"), [Arménio Vieira](/wiki/Arm%C3%A9nio_Vieira "Arménio Vieira"), Kaoberdiano Dambará, Dr. Azágua, and [Germano Almeida](/wiki/Germano_Almeida "Germano Almeida").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The first novel written by a woman from Cape Verde was *A Louca de Serrano* by [Dina Salústio](/wiki/Dina_Sal%C3%BAstio "Dina Salústio"); its translation, as *The Madwoman of Serrano,* was the first translation of any Cape Verdean novel to English.[[103]](#cite_note-:12-107)[[104]](#cite_note-:2-108)

### Cinema

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Cinema")]

The carnival and the island of São Vicente are portrayed in the 2015 feature documentary *[Tchindas](/wiki/Tchindas "Tchindas")*, nominated at the [12th Africa Movie Academy Awards](/wiki/12th_Africa_Movie_Academy_Awards "12th Africa Movie Academy Awards"). It was the location for the 2023 French film *[Ama Gloria](/wiki/Ama_Gloria "Ama Gloria")* by [Marie Amachoukeli](/wiki/Marie_Amachoukeli "Marie Amachoukeli").[[105]](#cite_note-109)

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Cachupa_2.jpg/250px-Cachupa_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cachupa_2.jpg)

Cachupa, typical Cape Verdean dish

[Cape Verdean cuisine](/wiki/Cape_Verdean_cuisine "Cape Verdean cuisine") is mostly based on fish and staple foods like corn and rice. Vegetables available during most of the year are potatoes, onions, tomatoes, [manioc](/wiki/Manioc "Manioc"), cabbage, kale, and dried beans. Fruits such as [bananas](/wiki/Banana "Banana") and [papayas](/wiki/Papaya "Papaya") are available year-round, while others like [mangoes](/wiki/Mango "Mango") and [avocados](/wiki/Avocado "Avocado") are seasonal.[[39]](#cite_note-pc-43)

A popular dish is *[cachupa](/wiki/Cachupa "Cachupa")*, a slow-cooked stew of corn (hominy), beans, and fish or meat. A common appetizer is the [*pastel*](/wiki/Pastel_(food) "Pastel (food)"), a pastry shell filled with fish or meat that is then fried.[[39]](#cite_note-pc-43)

### Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Sports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Est%C3%A1dio_Nacional_de_Cabo_Verde_panorama.jpg/500px-Est%C3%A1dio_Nacional_de_Cabo_Verde_panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Est%C3%A1dio_Nacional_de_Cabo_Verde_panorama.jpg)

[Estádio Nacional de Cabo Verde](/wiki/Est%C3%A1dio_Nacional_de_Cabo_Verde "Estádio Nacional de Cabo Verde") in [Praia](/wiki/Praia "Praia")

The most successful sports team is the [Cape Verde national basketball team](/wiki/Cape_Verde_national_basketball_team "Cape Verde national basketball team"), which won the bronze medal at the [FIBA Africa Championship 2007](/wiki/FIBA_Africa_Championship_2007 "FIBA Africa Championship 2007") after beating [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt_national_basketball_team "Egypt national basketball team") in its last game. They made their first appearance at the [FIBA Basketball World Cup](/wiki/FIBA_Basketball_World_Cup "FIBA Basketball World Cup") in [2023](/wiki/2023_FIBA_Basketball_World_Cup "2023 FIBA Basketball World Cup"). The country's most well-known player is [Walter Tavares](/wiki/Walter_Tavares "Walter Tavares"), who plays for [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_Baloncesto "Real Madrid Baloncesto") of Spain.

Cape Verde is famous for wave sailing[[106]](#cite_note-:4-110) (a type of [windsurfing](/wiki/Windsurfing "Windsurfing")) and [kiteboarding](/wiki/Kitesurfing "Kitesurfing").[[107]](#cite_note-111) Josh Angulo, a Hawaiian and 2009 PWA Wave World Champion, has done much to promote the archipelago as a windsurfing destination.[[106]](#cite_note-:4-110) Mitu Monteiro, a local kite-surfer was the 2008 [Kite Surfing World Champion](/wiki/International_Kiteboarding_Association#Wave_Riding "International Kiteboarding Association") in the wave discipline.

The [Cape Verde national football team](/wiki/Cape_Verde_national_football_team "Cape Verde national football team"), nicknamed the *Tubarões Azuis* (Blue Sharks), is the national team of Cape Verde and is controlled by the [Cape Verdean Football Federation](/wiki/Cape_Verdean_Football_Federation "Cape Verdean Football Federation"). The team has played at four [Africa Cup of Nations](/wiki/Africa_Cup_of_Nations "Africa Cup of Nations"), in [2013](/wiki/2013_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2013 Africa Cup of Nations"), [2015](/wiki/2015_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2015 Africa Cup of Nations"), [2021](/wiki/2021_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2021 Africa Cup of Nations") and [2023](/wiki/2023_Africa_Cup_of_Nations "2023 Africa Cup of Nations"),[[108]](#cite_note-112) and they will participate in their first [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") in [2026](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup "2026 FIFA World Cup").[[109]](#cite_note-113)

The country has competed at every [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Cape_Verde_at_the_Olympics "Cape Verde at the Olympics") since 1996; they had never won a medal until 2024, when [Daniel Varela de Pina](/wiki/Daniel_Varela_de_Pina "Daniel Varela de Pina") won a bronze medal in boxing. In 2016, [Gracelino Barbosa](/wiki/Gracelino_Barbosa "Gracelino Barbosa") became the first Cape Verdean to win a medal at the [Paralympic Games](/wiki/Paralympic_Games "Paralympic Games").[[110]](#cite_note-114)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Transport")]

Main article: [Transport in Cape Verde](/wiki/Transport_in_Cape_Verde "Transport in Cape Verde")

### Ports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Ports")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/CaisPortoNovo.JPG/250px-CaisPortoNovo.JPG)](/wiki/File:CaisPortoNovo.JPG)

Porto Novo harbour in Santo Antão

There are four international ports: [Mindelo](/wiki/Mindelo "Mindelo"), Praia, [Palmeira](/wiki/Palmeira,_Cape_Verde "Palmeira, Cape Verde"), and [Sal Rei](/wiki/Sal_Rei "Sal Rei"). Mindelo on São Vicente is the main port for cruise ships and the terminus for the ferry service to Santo Antão. Praia on Santiago is the main hub for local ferry services to other islands. Palmeira on Sal supplies fuel for the main airport on the island, Amílcar Cabral International Airport, and is important for the hotel construction taking place on the island. [Porto Novo](/wiki/Porto_Novo,_Cape_Verde "Porto Novo, Cape Verde") on Santo Antão is the only source for imports and exports of produce from the island, as well as passenger traffic, since the closure of the airstrip at [Ponta do Sol](/wiki/Ponta_do_Sol,_Cape_Verde "Ponta do Sol, Cape Verde").

There are smaller harbours, essentially single jetties at [Tarrafal](/wiki/Tarrafal,_Cape_Verde "Tarrafal, Cape Verde") on São Nicolau, Sal Rei on Boa Vista, [Vila do Maio (Porto Inglês)](/wiki/Porto_Ingl%C3%AAs "Porto Inglês") on Maio, [São Filipe](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Filipe,_Cape_Verde "São Filipe, Cape Verde") on Fogo and [Furna](/wiki/Furna_(Brava) "Furna (Brava)") on Brava. These act as terminals for the inter-island ferry services, which carry both freight and passengers. The pier at [Santa Maria](/wiki/Santa_Maria,_Cape_Verde "Santa Maria, Cape Verde") on Sal used by both fishing and dive boats has been rehabilitated.

### Airports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Airports")]

Main article: [List of airports in Cape Verde](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Cape_Verde "List of airports in Cape Verde")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Aeroporto-Internacional-Aristides-Pereira-Terminal-2012.JPG/250px-Aeroporto-Internacional-Aristides-Pereira-Terminal-2012.JPG)](/wiki/File:Aeroporto-Internacional-Aristides-Pereira-Terminal-2012.JPG)

[Aristides Pereira International Airport](/wiki/Aristides_Pereira_International_Airport "Aristides Pereira International Airport") in Boa Vista island

There were seven operational airports as of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit) – four international and three domestic. Two others were non-operational, one on Brava and the other on Santo Antão closed for safety reasons.

Due to its geographical location, Cape Verde is often flown over by transatlantic airliners. It is part of the conventional air traffic route from Europe to South America, which goes from southern Portugal via the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands") and Cape Verde to northern Brazil.

### Aerial drones

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cape_Verde&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Aerial drones")]

Small unmanned [flying drones](/wiki/Unmanned_aerial_vehicle "Unmanned aerial vehicle") able to carry up to 5 kg were being used experimentally for tasks such as delivering medicines between the islands in 2021.[[111]](#cite_note-115)
